explanation: Melia azadirachta (neem) leaf extract provides azadirachtin, nimbin, and polyphenol antioxidants with potent antibacterial, antifungal, anti-inflammatory, and free-radical-scavenging properties. It is well-tolerated at extract concentrations and used in purifying, anti-acne, and soothing formulas for its broad-spectrum antimicrobial and antioxidant content.

Mentha arvensis (field mint/corn mint) extract provides polyphenol antioxidants and menthol precursor compounds with mild antimicrobial and refreshing properties. The extract form is considerably gentler than the essential oil and is generally well-tolerated, used in cooling and purifying formulas for its antioxidant and antimicrobial content.

Mentha arvensis (corn mint) leaf oil is a natural essential oil with very high menthol content, used for its strong cooling scent but carrying documented contact sensitization and irritation risk. It can cause stinging, burning, and allergic contact reactions on reactive or fragrance-sensitive skin, and is best avoided in leave-on products for sensitive skin.

Mentha piperita (peppermint) extract provides polyphenol antioxidants including rosmarinic acid and luteolin with antimicrobial and free-radical-scavenging properties. The extract form carries significantly lower sensitization and irritation risk than peppermint essential oil, and is generally well-tolerated in formulas for its antioxidant and mildly refreshing properties.

Menthol is a cyclic terpene alcohol that creates a cooling sensation by activating TRPM8 cold receptors in the skin without actually lowering temperature. At typical cosmetic concentrations it is generally well-tolerated, though it can cause stinging, flushing, and irritation on reactive or barrier-compromised skin — particularly in leave-on products — and is best approached with caution by those with fragrance sensitivity.

Menthyl lactate is an ester of menthol and lactic acid that provides a milder, longer-lasting cooling sensation than menthol with lower irritation potential. It is generally well-tolerated at typical cosmetic concentrations and used in cooling and refreshing formulas as a gentler alternative to raw menthol.

Methicone is a low-molecular-weight monomethyl silicone used as a lightweight spreading and smoothing agent in skincare formulas, providing less occlusive coverage than longer-chain dimethicone variants. It is considered well-tolerated, non-sensitizing, and used in lightweight moisturizers and primers for its silky skin feel.

Methyl gluceth-20 is a PEG-modified methyl glucoside derivative used as a mild humectant and emollient, providing moisture-binding and skin-conditioning properties with a lighter feel than pure glycerin. It is generally well-tolerated, though individuals with PEG sensitivity should note the polyethylene glycol modification.

Methyl trimethicone is a low-viscosity, volatile silicone used as a lightweight spreading and film-forming agent in primers and makeup formulas, evaporating after application to leave a smooth, non-greasy finish. It is considered well-tolerated, non-sensitizing, and non-comedogenic.

Methyl/methacrylate copolymer is a synthetic acrylate polymer used as a film-former in long-wear makeup and nail formulas, providing durable, water-resistant coverage. It is considered well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic use concentrations, functioning as a texture and durability modifier.

Methylchloroisothiazolinone (MCI) is a potent biocide preservative and well-documented contact allergen responsible for a significant epidemic of allergic contact dermatitis. The EU has banned MCI/MIT in leave-on products and restricted it in rinse-off products; it can trigger allergic reactions and an itch-and-scratch cycle with repeated exposure and is best avoided by anyone with reactive or sensitized skin.

Methylchloroisothiazolinone (MCI) is a potent biocide preservative and well-documented contact allergen responsible for a significant epidemic of allergic contact dermatitis. The EU has banned it in leave-on products; it can trigger allergic reactions and an itch-and-scratch cycle with repeated exposure and is best avoided by anyone with reactive or sensitized skin.

Methylisothiazolinone (MIT) is a biocide preservative that caused a significant global epidemic of allergic contact dermatitis — its use in leave-on products is banned in the EU and restricted in rinse-off products. It is a potent contact allergen that can trigger allergic reactions and an itch-and-scratch cycle with repeated exposure; reactive and fragrance-sensitive individuals should avoid it entirely.

Methylparaben is the most commonly used paraben preservative, with scrutiny for potential weak estrogenic activity in laboratory studies and is a known contact sensitizer in individuals with paraben sensitivity. Regulatory bodies consider it safe at approved concentrations, though it is commonly avoided by reactive skin users and increasingly replaced in clean-formula skincare.

Mica is a naturally occurring mineral silicate used in cosmetics to impart shimmer, luminosity, and soft-focus effects in foundations, highlighters, and eyeshadows. It is considered inert, non-sensitizing, and well-tolerated — one of the most skin-compatible mineral pigments available, approved for all cosmetic applications including eye area products.

Microcrystalline cellulose is a refined, purified plant cellulose used in skincare and pharmaceutical formulas as a thickener, anti-caking agent, and suspension stabilizer. It is considered inert, non-sensitizing, and exceptionally well-tolerated across all skin types — functioning as a texture modifier rather than a skin-active ingredient.

Milk thistle (Silybum marianum) extract is rich in silymarin — a complex of flavonolignans including silybin — with potent antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and photoprotective properties. It is well-tolerated, non-sensitizing, and used in protective and anti-aging formulas for its documented ability to neutralize free radicals and reduce UV-induced oxidative stress.

Montmorillonite is a swelling smectite clay mineral with high absorptive capacity used in face masks and cleansers to bind excess sebum, toxins, and impurities from the skin surface. It is generally well-tolerated and non-sensitizing, though its strong oil-absorbing properties can be drying with repeated use on dry or sensitive skin types.

Morinda citrifolia (noni) seed oil is a plant oil rich in linoleic acid and tocopherols with barrier-repairing and antioxidant properties. It is well-tolerated and used in nourishing and protective formulas for its emollient and antioxidant content.

Moringa oleifera (moringa) seed oil is rich in oleic acid (~70%), behenic acid, and tocopherols with excellent emollient, antioxidant, and skin-conditioning properties. It is well-tolerated, has low comedogenic potential, and is used in moisturizers and hair care formulas for its nourishing fatty acid profile and exceptional oxidative stability.

Myristic acid is a 14-carbon saturated fatty acid naturally occurring in many plant and animal fats, used in skincare as an emollient and saponification ingredient in cleansers. It has moderate comedogenic potential and is generally well-tolerated as an emollient, though its pore-clogging tendency makes it less suitable for acne-prone skin at higher concentrations.

Myristyl alcohol is a 14-carbon fatty alcohol used as an emollient and co-emulsifier in skincare formulas, contributing a rich, smooth skin feel and helping to stabilize emulsions. It is well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic use concentrations.

N-acetyl-D-glucosamine (NAG) is an amino sugar and hyaluronic acid precursor used in skincare as a mild brightening agent, gentle exfoliant, and moisture-supporting active that also inhibits tyrosinase activity to reduce melanin production. It is well-tolerated, non-irritating, and works synergistically with niacinamide in brightening and anti-aging formulas.

N-decanoyl serinol (NDCS) is an amino acid-derived lipid that structurally resembles sphingosine and supports the skin's lipid barrier by integrating into the lamellar lipid structure of the stratum corneum. It is well-tolerated and used in barrier-repair and sensitive skin formulas for its ceramide-analogue barrier-reinforcing properties.

Nelumbo nucifera (sacred lotus) extract provides flavonoids, alkaloids, and polyphenol antioxidants with anti-inflammatory, brightening, and free-radical-scavenging properties. It is well-tolerated and used in brightening and protective formulas for its antioxidant and skin-conditioning content.

Nelumbo nucifera (sacred lotus) flower extract provides quercetin, luteolin, and polyphenol antioxidants with anti-inflammatory and brightening properties. It is well-tolerated and used in brightening and protective formulas for its antioxidant and skin-illuminating content.

Nelumbo nucifera (sacred lotus) root extract provides polyphenols and alkaloids with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, traditionally used in East Asian skincare. It is well-tolerated and used in protective and brightening formulas for its antioxidant content.

Neopentyl glycol diethylhexanoate is a lightweight branched-chain ester emollient providing a dry, non-greasy skin feel with good spreadability and oxidative stability. It is well-tolerated, non-comedogenic, and used in lightweight moisturizers and sunscreens as a comfortable skin-feel enhancer.

Neopentyl glycol diheptanoate is a synthetic branched-chain ester emollient providing a dry, silky skin feel with low viscosity and good spreading properties. It is well-tolerated, non-comedogenic, and used in lightweight moisturizers and sunscreens as a skin-feel modifier.

Niacinamide (vitamin B3) is one of the most versatile and well-researched actives in skincare, with documented benefits including inhibition of melanosome transfer (brightening), reduction of sebum production, reinforcement of the skin barrier via ceramide synthesis, and anti-inflammatory action for acne-prone skin. It is exceptionally well-tolerated across all skin types including sensitive and acne-prone, with rare flushing reactions reported only at very high concentrations (>10%) in susceptible individuals.
